summarization.a: AFM image of the initial AlGaN surface, with RMS roughness 0.4 nm over a 5 x 5 μm area.
summarization.b: AFM image after Cl2 ALE, showing a rougher surface with RMS roughness 1.8 nm.
summarization.c: AFM image after BCl3 and Cl2 ALE, showing a smoother surface with RMS roughness 0.3 nm.

data_extraction.a:
| Figure | Description |
| --- | --- |
| (a) | 5 x 5 µm image with RMS = 0.4 nm |
| (b) | Image with RMS = 1.8 nm |
| (c) | Image with RMS = 0.3 nm |

c.question: How does surface roughness change from (a) to (b) to (c)?
c.answer: The initial surface in (a) has moderate roughness at 0.4 nm. After Cl2 ALE in (b) the roughness increases to 1.8 nm, indicating strong roughening. After BCl3 and Cl2 ALE in (c) the roughness decreases to 0.3 nm, indicating a smoother surface than the initial case.

c.question: Which ALE chemistry produces the smoothest AlGaN surface in this figure?
c.answer: BCl3 and Cl2 ALE, shown in panel (c) with RMS roughness 0.3 nm.

a.question: What does the RMS value shown on each AFM panel represent?
a.answer: It is the RMS surface roughness of the AlGaN surface in nm for that scan area.

a.question: Why might lower AlGaN surface roughness after etching be important for device processing?
a.answer:
It can help maintain uniform electric fields in near surface device regions.

It can improve interface quality for later dielectric or metal deposition.

It can reduce variability in critical dimensions for patterned features.
